g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Creating Member Operator Functions in C++. This is a powerful feature of OOP.
g.text: Operator Overloading in C++ - university 2022 Scheme

g.data-narration: Operator overloading lets us redefine existing operators like + and - to work with our custom classes, making code intuitive.
Member operator functions have implicit access to class members via the this pointer, perfect when the left operand is your class object.
g.text: ✓ Perfect for left operand = class object

g.data-narration: The syntax uses the operator keyword followed by the operator symbol. Binary takes 1 argument, unary takes none.
Here's a Complex number class with the + operator overloaded as a member function, demonstrating how it works internally.
For binary operators as members, the left operand becomes the calling object via this pointer, and right operand is passed as parameter.
Unary operators like ++, --, -, ! take NO arguments as member functions. The operand is the object itself, accessed via this.
The post-increment uses a dummy int parameter to distinguish from pre-increment. This is a key exam point.
g.text: parameter is required to distinguish post-increment. Without it, compilation error!

g.data-narration: Certain operators cannot be overloaded in C++: scope resolution ::, member access ., .*, ternary ?:, and sizeof.
Use member functions when left operand is your class object. Use friend functions when left operand is NOT your class, like cout << obj.
g.text: Left operand = object of your class
should preferably be member functions (not friend)

g.data-narration: The assignment operator must check for self-assignment and perform deep copy for classes with pointers. Always return *this for chaining.
g.text: ✓ Deep copy: delete old, allocate new

g.data-narration: Let's see a complete example: a Distance class that overloads + and - operators to add and subtract measurements.
Here's how to overload comparison operators == and != for a Matrix class to compare 2x2 matrices element by element.
The subscript operator [] can be overloaded to provide bounds checking, returning a reference for assignment operations.
g.text: Example 3: IntArray Class ([] with bounds check)
Returns reference for LHS, value for RHS

g.data-narration: Always mark operator functions as const when they don't modify the object. This allows them to work with const objects too.
g.text: for operators that don't modify the object

g.data-narration: Key exam tips: Binary member = 1 arg, unary = 0 args, post-increment needs dummy int, return *this from = operator, cannot overload :: . ?: sizeof.
